algorithms.title: Pediatric Shock Recognition and Classification
algorithms.description: A systematic approach to identifying, staging, and classifying shock in pediatric patients, from initial assessment through type determination and immediate intervention.
steps: Perform rapid assessment using the Pediatric Assessment Triangle: evaluate appearance, work of breathing, and circulation to skin. Pallor, mottling, or cyanosis in circulation to skin suggests shock.
Assess perfusion: check capillary refill time (sternum or fingertip), pulse quality (strong vs weak/thready), skin temperature and color, mental status, and urine output.
Obtain blood pressure and apply the age-appropriate hypotension formula: SBP < 70 + (2 × age in years) for children 1–10 years; SBP < 70 mmHg for infants.

Classify the stage of shock:
  • Compensated: tachycardia, weak pulses, prolonged CRT, cool/mottled skin, altered mental status, but BP is maintained.
  • Decompensated: worsening perfusion, BP beginning to fall, mental status deteriorating.
  • Hypotensive: BP below age-appropriate threshold — child is near cardiac arrest.

Determine the TYPE of shock:
  • Blood loss, dehydration, third-spacing? → Hypovolemic
  • Fever, infection, warm skin early then cold? → Distributive (septic)
  • Urticaria, angioedema, wheezing after allergen? → Distributive (anaphylactic)
  • Spinal cord injury + bradycardia + hypotension? → Distributive (neurogenic)
  • Gallop, hepatomegaly, pulmonary edema? → Cardiogenic
  • Muffled heart sounds, JVD, tracheal deviation? → Obstructive

Initiate immediate treatment:
  • IV/IO access — at least 2 large-bore peripheral lines or intraosseous access.
  • Isotonic crystalloid bolus 10–20 mL/kg (cautious in cardiogenic shock).
  • Reassess after each bolus: perfusion, heart rate, mental status, blood pressure.
Continuously reassess: if the child is not improving or is worsening after two fluid boluses, consider vasoactive support and reassess the type and stage of shock.
